name: Shared Resource Pool
description: Multiple players contribute to and spend from a shared pool of currency, materials, charges, or objective resources.
design_notes: Shared resources need atomic transactions and visible accountability. Designers should define shared_resource_id ownership, contribution logs, spend permissions, ordering, rollback on failed spends, and anti-grief rules so one player cannot silently drain team progress.
design_purpose: Encourage teamwork by turning individual gains into group capabilities.
Support shared base upgrades, team abilities, objective payments, or communal survival stockpiles.
Reduce loot conflict when cooperation matters more than individual ownership.
player_fantasy: Players feel that their contributions matter to a shared plan and that group spending is trustworthy.

parameters.name: shared_resource_id
parameters.description: Pool identifier used by contributions, spends, UI, persistence, and permission checks.

parameters.name: contribution_log_id
parameters.description: Audit log for player_id, source, quantity, and tick of each accepted contribution.

parameters.name: spend_request_id
parameters.description: Transaction identifier used to authorize, reject, rollback, or de-duplicate a spend.

parameters.name: permission_policy_id
parameters.description: Rule that decides which players may spend, reserve, withdraw, or reassign the shared pool.

parameters.name: spend_order_index
parameters.description: Monotonic transaction index that prevents simultaneous spends from consuming the same pool units twice.

parameters.name: anti_grief_rule_id
parameters.description: Rule used to reduce malicious or accidental depletion of shared resources.

edge_cases: Two players spend the last available resource units on different upgrades during the same server tick.
A player disconnects after reserving resources but before the target craft, vote, or upgrade completes.
A party member leaves and the game must decide whether their contributions stay with the team.
Late joiners need pool totals and contribution history without replaying every old event.
common_bugs: Clients subtract shared resources locally and later receive a server correction that makes UI totals jump backward.
Failed spend requests do not release reserved resources, permanently reducing the visible pool.
Contribution logs use display names instead of player_id and cannot survive renames or reconnects.
Permission checks happen in UI only, allowing direct network requests to spend restricted resources.
balancing_notes: Shared pools reduce individual hoarding but can create social friction if spending rights are unclear.
Large purchases benefit from confirmation, vote, or role restrictions so one player cannot erase group progress.
Contribution visibility helps players feel credited without turning cooperation into a blame scoreboard.
accessibility_notes: Show pool changes with readable text, contribution source, and spend reason for players who miss transient notifications.
Provide non-voice approval flows for vote-required spending.

implementation_notes.unity: Store shared_resource_id state in a server-owned service, not on individual UI widgets. Spend RPCs should include spend_request_id, expected spend_order_index, player_id, and permission policy; the server commits the pool mutation and contribution log together.
implementation_notes.godot: Use an authoritative resource service keyed by shared_resource_id and expose snapshots to UI. RPCs for contribute, reserve, spend, and cancel should all pass through one transaction path that records contribution_log_id entries.
implementation_notes.unreal: A replicated team state actor or subsystem can own shared resource totals and contribution logs. Server RPCs validate permission_policy_id and anti_grief_rule_id before mutating the pool, then replicate versioned totals to clients.
implementation_notes.web: Keep shared pool transactions on the backend or host authority with idempotent spend_request_id values. Browser clients should display optimistic pending spends separately from committed totals to avoid confusing correction jumps.

relationships.reason: Shared resource pools often spend team currency or pooled materials through ordered transactions.
Shared resources reinforce team objectives by making contributions and spending visible to the group.
Shared resources and friendly fire both need anti-grief rules because one player can harm group progress.

example_games.title: Deep Rock Galactic
example_games.note: Uses shared team resources during missions for support actions.

example_games.title: Helldivers 2
example_games.note: Uses team-level mission resources and cooperative objective pressure.
